 {"id":"bd-1k7c5","title":"Perf: Profile + Optimize Layout/Render Hotspots","description":"Background
- Layout and routing can be CPU-heavy on large graphs.
- Must follow extreme optimization loop: baseline → profile → single lever → verify.

Scope
- Baseline runtime with hyperfine for large diagrams.
- Profile hotspots (cargo flamegraph or perf).
- Opportunity matrix; implement only score ≥ 2.0 changes.
- Verify deterministic output via golden checksums.
- Track memory allocations and cache hit rates.

Considerations
- One lever per change; no behavior drift.
- Capture before/after metrics in comments or logs.

Success Criteria
- Measured speedup without output changes.
- Golden checksum verification passes.","notes":"Progress 2026-02-05: fixed clippy in mermaid_render (if-let chains for selection highlight + tests; removed unused imports). Fixed mermaid_showcase compile issues (import mermaid module; use std::time::Instant via fully qualified paths). Quality gates: cargo clippy/check/fmt --check all pass. Latest perf snapshot (from prior run): hyperfine mean ~171.7ms for perf_large_graph_layout; alloc stats: allocs 1,317,200, bytes_allocated 664,858,000, reallocs 424,000, bytes_reallocated 123,062,400; perf report hotspots: evaluate_layout ~19.9%, Vec<usize>::extend_with ~16%, malloc_consolidate ~10%.
Progress 2026-02-05: set Mermaid render default to Braille (MermaidConfig default + mermaid_showcase/mermaid_mega_showcase defaults + updated tests). Fixed clippy collapsible-if in mermaid_mega_showcase tests and adjusted mermaid_layout free-spot scan (array candidates) to satisfy clippy/compile. Quality gates: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings all pass.

Progress 2026-02-06: mermaid_layout micro-alloc cleanup (no behavior change):
- Avoid building node_id_map HashMap when constraints are empty.
- Avoid allocating a temporary Vec in compute_bounding_box.

perf_large_graph_layout (release, 200 runs):
- allocs: 1,018,600 -> 1,018,200
- bytes_allocated: 215,574,000 -> 212,530,800
- reallocs: 424,000 (unchanged)
- checksum: 0x00000000004dff30 (unchanged)

 {"id":"bd-1k7ek.1","title":"task(pane-perf): build long-lived pane-core profiling harness with symbolized replay artifacts","description":"Goal\n- Replace thin short-lived benchmark sampling with a repeatable profiling harness that keeps the pane timeline replay path hot long enough to produce trustworthy CPU/allocation evidence.\n\nWhy this exists\n- Recent pane-core optimization attempts showed that Criterion exact runs are too short for rich stack attribution even after enabling debug symbols in bench builds. Without denser samples, future work risks chasing noise or optimizing the wrong layer.\n\nScope\n- Add a long-lived replay-focused harness for pane-core structural workloads.\n- Ensure local bench/profile binaries retain symbols needed by perf and similar tooling.\n- Capture reproducible golden outputs and state hashes for the profiled scenarios.\n- Emit artifact paths and run metadata suitable for CI and local triage.\n\nDeliverables\n- Long-lived pane replay harness and invocation docs.\n- Symbolized artifact path convention for pane perf work.\n- Before/after counter/profile capture recipe.\n- Golden snapshot/state-hash fixtures for the harness scenarios.\n\nAcceptance\n- Profiling a replay-heavy scenario yields materially richer evidence than the short Criterion exact path.\n- The harness is deterministic enough to support future optimization proofs and CI reuse.","acceptance_criteria":"1) The harness produces deterministic long-history workloads with reproducible state hashes and golden outputs.
2) It emits operator-friendly artifact manifests, run metadata, and verbose logging sufficient to compare baseline, checkpointed, persistent, and policy-selected strategies.
3) It includes regression checks or self-tests that fail if symbolization, artifact emission, or scenario determinism silently degrade.
4) The harness is documented well enough for CI and local profiling reuse without rediscovering setup steps.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:33:46.142780783Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","updated_at":"2026-03-15T01:53:48.792667565Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T01:53:45.526433353Z","close_reason":"Implemented long-lived pane-core profiling harness with local artifact reconstruction and docs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["pane","perf","profiling","symbols","timeline"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:33:46.142780783Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1061,"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.1","author":"AmberBadger","text":"Background and intent: this bead is the evidence foundation for every later optimization in the pane-core lane. The measured failure signature is not merely 'slow code' but 'insufficient profiling fidelity for asymptotic decisions'. The harness should therefore focus on representative long-history workloads, preserve deterministic state hashes, and make it trivial to compare baseline, checkpointed, and persistent execution models under the same scenario.","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:34:06Z"},{"id":1092,"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.1","author":"ubuntu","text":"Implementation progress: added a new harness=false bench target `pane_profile_harness` in ftui-layout, wired `scripts/pane_profile.sh` to invoke it, and verified local reconstruction of `manifest.json`, `baseline_snapshot.json`, `final_snapshot.json`, and `run.log` under `target/pane-profiling/...` even when the harness executes remotely via `rch`. This closes the first concrete gap in the bead: long-lived deterministic pane-core profiling no longer depends on short Criterion runs or remote-only artifacts.","created_at":"2026-03-15T00:46:07Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1k7ek.2","title":"task(pane-perf): implement checkpointed PaneInteractionTimeline with analytically tuned rebuild interval","description":"Goal\n- Achieve the first major asymptotic replay win without a full persistent-tree rewrite by inserting deterministic checkpoints into PaneInteractionTimeline and rebuilding from the nearest checkpoint instead of always from the baseline.\n\nWhy this exists\n- The current replay cost grows with applied history length. Checkpointing is the lowest-risk architectural lever that can cut replay depth dramatically while preserving the existing conceptual model.\n\nDesign expectations\n- Maintain deterministic semantics and cursor behavior for apply/undo/redo/replay.\n- Select checkpoint spacing from measured costs rather than a hand-wavy constant.\n- Record enough metadata to explain why a given interval was chosen.\n\nDeliverables\n- Checkpoint-aware timeline implementation.\n- Interval-selection note or artifact based on measured replay vs snapshot costs.\n- Benchmarks for short, medium, and long histories.\n- Behavior-proof fixtures covering undo/redo/replay equivalence.\n\nAcceptance\n- Long-history replay is measurably faster than baseline replay.\n- Deterministic replay outputs and hashes are unchanged.\n- Memory growth is bounded or explicitly deferred to the retention-policy workstream.","acceptance_criteria":"Unit tests cover checkpoint creation, replay-from-nearest-checkpoint logic, cursor semantics, checkpoint-spacing selection decisions, and edge cases around short and long histories. Integration tests prove replay, undo, redo, and state-hash equivalence with the baseline timeline on representative and adversarial histories. Benchmark and profiling artifacts include detailed timing, replay-depth, checkpoint-hit, spacing-decision, and fallback-to-conservative-path logging sufficient for CI and local triage. Any memory-growth tradeoff is either bounded in implementation or explicitly logged and deferred to the retention-policy bead with clear evidence. Operators and developers can understand why a checkpoint interval was chosen, when conservative replay behavior was forced, and how that affected responsiveness and artifact output.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:34:06.653551262Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","updated_at":"2026-03-15T02:03:35.224882460Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithmic","checkpointing","pane","perf","timeline"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:34:06.653551262Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:40:05.924975324Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1062,"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.2","author":"AmberBadger","text":"Reasoning/justification: this is the highest-EV intermediate step between today's baseline replay and a fully persistent versioned tree.  {"id":"bd-1k7ek.3","title":"task(pane-perf): add certified operation-family fast paths for local pane mutations","description":"Goal\n- Partition pane operations into semantically meaningful families and give the local families optimized execution paths that avoid generic full-transaction costs when proof obligations allow it.\n\nWhy this exists\n- Operations like local ratio changes and tightly bounded topology edits should not always pay the same machinery as globally disruptive edits. The measured opportunity is to classify operation families and optimize the low-risk local ones with explicit equivalence evidence.\n\nScope\n- Define the operation-family classifier.\n- Identify candidate local families and their preconditions.\n- Implement optimized execution paths that preserve canonical outputs.\n- Attach proof artifacts or differential tests showing equivalence to the baseline path.\n\nDeliverables\n- Operation-family inventory and classifier.\n- One or more optimized local fast paths.\n- Differential/golden tests and counterexample fixtures.\n- Before/after benchmark deltas tied to each family.\n\nAcceptance\n- Local families show measurable wins.\n- Fallback to the baseline path remains deterministic and easy to audit.\n- Unsound preconditions are guarded by explicit tests or proof fixtures.","acceptance_criteria":"1) Unit tests cover operation-family classification, precondition guards, and deterministic fallback to the baseline path.
2) Integration and differential tests prove semantic equivalence for each optimized family, including counterexample fixtures for invalid fast-path entry.
3) Benchmark artifacts and detailed logs explain per-family wins, fallback frequency, and any mismatches.
4) Each fast path has an explicit proof story and isomorphism rationale, not just a speed result.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:34:27.361847925Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","updated_at":"2026-03-14T04:57:52.512527322Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["certified-rewrite","fast-paths","optimization","pane","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:34:27.361847925Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:40:10.952008297Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:40:15.967111665Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1063,"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.3","author":"AmberBadger","text":"Intent: this bead is the practical application of certified-rewrite thinking to the pane engine. The standard to meet is not just 'faster on benchmarks' but 'faster because a smaller semantic class admits a cheaper, equivalence-checked implementation'. Future work should reject ornamental fast paths that lack a clear operation-family boundary or baseline differential proof.","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:40:03Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1k7ek.4","title":"task(pane-perf): implement incremental invariant-closure validation for pane mutations","description":"Goal\n- Replace blanket whole-tree post-operation validation on eligible mutation families with a local closure validator that rechecks only the affected invariants and their dependency neighborhood.\n\nWhy this exists\n- Full-tree validation is correct but often over-conservative for local edits. The pane core already exposes touched-node information; this bead turns that into a real local-to-global consistency engine.\n\nScope\n- Inventory pane-tree invariants and the nodes/relationships each invariant depends on.\n- Build a dependency closure or touched-region validator.\n- Define escalation rules for operations that cannot yet be proven locally safe.\n- Preserve the baseline full validator as the conservative fallback.\n\nDeliverables\n- Invariant dependency map.\n- Local validation closure engine.\n- Fallback/escalation policy for uncertain operations.\n- Proof-style tests showing local validation preserves global validity.\n\nAcceptance\n- Eligible local operations avoid unnecessary global validation.\n- Non-eligible operations deterministically escalate to the baseline validator.\n- No correctness regression is introduced in replay, undo/redo, or snapshot export.","acceptance_criteria":"1) Unit tests cover invariant-dependency mapping, touched-region closure computation, and escalation triggers to the whole-tree validator.
2) Integration tests prove local validation preserves global correctness for eligible operations and that unsafe cases escalate deterministically.
3) Failure logging identifies the invariant set, touched region, and escalation decision for debugging.
4) The conservative full validator remains available and easy to force during diagnosis, rollback, and rollout.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:36:32.813173653Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","updated_at":"2026-03-14T04:57:52.545340456Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["invariants","locality","pane","perf","validation"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:36:32.813173653Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1k7ek.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:40:20.978265547Z","created_by":"AmberBadger","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1064,"issue_id":"bd-1k7ek.4","author":"AmberBadger","text":"Background: this is the local-to-global consistency bead. Fast paths become much more compelling once their correctness no longer depends on revalidating the entire tree after every small mutation. Future-self rule: if the closure is not obviously complete, escalate to whole-tree validation rather than smuggling optimism into correctness.","created_at":"2026-03-14T04:40:03Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1k7ek.5","title":"task(pane-perf): prototype persistent/versioned PaneTree for replay-free undo and redo","description":"Goal
- Explore the highest-ceiling architectural optimization: persistent/versioned pane trees where each structural change produces a new root with structural sharing, so undo/redo can move between versions instead of reconstructing history.

Why this exists
- Checkpointing can reduce replay cost, but persistence can eliminate most replay work entirely for version navigation. This is the likely path to the biggest long-term gains if the memory and complexity trade-offs are acceptable.

Scope
- Prototype versioned roots and structural sharing for representative pane operations.
- Evaluate the effect on replay, undo/redo, snapshot export, and state-hash computation.
- Compare memory behavior against checkpointed timelines.
- Produce a clear go/no-go recommendation for full adoption.

Deliverables
- Prototype versioned pane-tree machinery or focused spike.
- Benchmark comparison against baseline and checkpointed replay.
- Memory-retention analysis and risks.
- Explicit adoption recommendation with rollback posture.

Acceptance
- The prototype yields clear evidence on whether persistence is worth promoting.
- Behavior remains deterministic on the exercised path.
- The trade-off between speed, memory, and implementation complexity is documented in the bead itself.","acceptance_criteria":"1) The prototype includes unit and integration coverage for version navigation, structural sharing invariants, state-hash stability, and rollback to baseline behavior on the exercised path.
2) It emits detailed memory-retention, version-count, and replay/undo timing logs so the adoption decision is evidence-backed rather than intuitive.
3) It produces a clear go/no-go recommendation with explicit user/operator implications, including debugging ergonomics and rollback posture.

 {"id":"bd-1xo","title":"Windows Terminal compatibility layer","description":"PHASE 7 - POLISH: WINDOWS SUPPORT

## Background
Windows Terminal v1 support is deferred but must have clear integration path. Modern Windows Terminal supports ANSI, older conhost needs different handling.

## Technical Requirements (v1 Scope - Windows Terminal Only)
- Detect Windows Terminal vs legacy conhost
- Enable VT processing mode via SetConsoleMode
- Handle Windows-specific escape sequences
- PTY spawning via ConPTY API
- Signal handling differences (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+Break)
- Path handling for Windows conventions

## Out of Scope for v1
- Legacy conhost support (Windows 7/8)
- PowerShell-specific handling
- Windows-specific widgets

## Design Rationale
Per ADR-004, Windows v1 targets modern Windows Terminal only. This dramatically simplifies implementation while covering majority of Windows developer use.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Windows Terminal detection
- [ ] VT mode enabling
- [ ] ConPTY integration for PTY tests
- [ ] Signal handling shim
- [ ] CI testing on Windows
- [ ] Feature gate: windows

## Testing
- GitHub Actions Windows runner
- Manual testing on Windows Terminal
- Document known limitations

 {"id":"bd-cyww","title":"Define DegradationLevel enum with 6 progressive levels","description":"# Define DegradationLevel enum with 6 progressive levels

## Background
Part of Render Budget Enforcement (bd-1iqn). Progressive degradation levels allow the system to maintain responsiveness by reducing visual fidelity under load.

## Technical Design

```rust
/// Progressive degradation levels for render budget enforcement
/// Levels are ordered from highest fidelity to lowest
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Debug, Default)]
#[repr(u8)]
pub enum DegradationLevel {
    /// Full fidelity - all visual features enabled
    /// Unicode box-drawing, full styling, animations
    #[default]
    Full = 0,
    
    /// Skip expensive borders (Unicode box-drawing → ASCII +-)
    /// Still has colors and styling, just simpler borders
    SimpleBorders = 1,
    
    /// Skip styling (colors → monochrome)
    /// Text is readable but no color differentiation
    NoStyling = 2,
    
    /// Skip non-essential widgets (decorative only)
    /// Only functional content rendered
    EssentialOnly = 3,
    
    /// Skeleton render (just layout boxes)
    /// Shows structure but no content
    Skeleton = 4,
    
    /// Emergency: skip frame entirely
    /// Used when system is severely overloaded
    SkipFrame = 5,
}
```

## Implementation Details

```rust
impl DegradationLevel {
    /// Move to next degradation level (lower fidelity)
    pub fn degrade(&self) -> Self {
        match self {
            Self::Full => Self::SimpleBorders,
            Self::SimpleBorders => Self::NoStyling,
            Self::NoStyling => Self::EssentialOnly,
            Self::EssentialOnly => Self::Skeleton,
            Self::Skeleton | Self::SkipFrame => Self::SkipFrame,
        }
    }
    
    /// Move to previous degradation level (higher fidelity)
    pub fn upgrade(&self) -> Self {
        match self {
            Self::SkipFrame => Self::Skeleton,
            Self::Skeleton => Self::EssentialOnly,
            Self::EssentialOnly => Self::NoStyling,
            Self::NoStyling => Self::SimpleBorders,
            Self::SimpleBorders | Self::Full => Self::Full,
        }
    }
    
    /// Check if borders should use ASCII instead of Unicode
    pub fn use_ascii_borders(&self) -> bool {
        *self >= Self::SimpleBorders
    }
    
    /// Check if styling (colors) should be applied
    pub fn apply_styling(&self) -> bool {
        *self < Self::NoStyling
    }
    
    /// Check if non-essential widgets should render
    pub fn render_decorative(&self) -> bool {
        *self < Self::EssentialOnly
    }
    
    /// Check if content should render (vs skeleton)
    pub fn render_content(&self) -> bool {
        *self < Self::Skeleton
    }
    
    /// Check if frame should be skipped entirely
    pub fn skip_frame(&self) -> bool {
        *self >= Self::SkipFrame
    }
}
```

## Why This Design

1. **Ordered enum**: Levels are comparable (Full < SimpleBorders < ...)
2. **Bounded transitions**: degrade() and upgrade() never go out of bounds
3. **Query methods**: Widgets can easily check what features to enable
4. **Default = Full**: System starts at highest fidelity

## Test Requirements

```rust
#[test]
fn degradation_level_ordering() {
    assert!(DegradationLevel::Full < DegradationLevel::SimpleBorders);
    assert!(DegradationLevel::SimpleBorders < DegradationLevel::NoStyling);
    // ... all pairs
}

#[test]
fn degrade_never_goes_beyond_skip_frame() {
    let mut level = DegradationLevel::SkipFrame;
    for _ in 0..10 {
        level = level.degrade();
        assert_eq!(level, DegradationLevel::SkipFrame);
    }
}

#[test]
fn upgrade_never_goes_beyond_full() {
    let mut level = DegradationLevel::Full;
    for _ in 0..10 {
        level = level.upgrade();
        assert_eq!(level, DegradationLevel::Full);
    }
}

#[test]
fn query_methods_match_level() {
    assert!(!DegradationLevel::Full.use_ascii_borders());
    assert!(DegradationLevel::SimpleBorders.use_ascii_borders());
    // ... etc
}
```

- system_recovers_after_budget_exhaustion: After overload, system eventually upgrades back

+symbolize_perf_user_frames() {
+    local perf_data="$1"
+    local binary="$2"
+    local output_file="$3"
+
+    python3 - "$perf_data" "$binary" "$output_file" <<'PY'
+import collections
+import pathlib
+import re
+import subprocess
+import sys
+
+perf_data = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[1])
+binary = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[2]).resolve()
+output_file = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[3])
+
+script = subprocess.check_output(
+    ["perf", "script", "--show-mmap-events", "-i", str(perf_data)],
+    text=True,
+    errors="replace",
+)
+
+mmap_re = re.compile(
+    r"PERF_RECORD_MMAP2 .*: \[(0x[0-9a-f]+)\(0x[0-9a-f]+\) @ (0x[0-9a-f]+) <[^>]+>\]: [^ ]+ (.+)$"
+)
+symbolized_frame_re = re.compile(r"^\s*([0-9a-f]+)\s+(.+)\s+\((.+)\)$")
+
+mmaps = []
+weighted_symbols = collections.Counter()
+for line in script.splitlines():
+    match = mmap_re.search(line)
+    if not match:
+        continue
+    path = pathlib.Path(match.group(3)).resolve()
+    if path != binary:
+        continue
+    mmaps.append((int(match.group(1), 16), int(match.group(2), 16)))
+
+counter = collections.Counter()
+for line in script.splitlines():
+    symbol_match = symbolized_frame_re.match(line)
+    if not symbol_match:
+        continue
+    path = pathlib.Path(symbol_match.group(3)).resolve()
+    if path != binary:
+        continue
+    symbol = symbol_match.group(2).strip()
+    if symbol != "[unknown]":
+        weighted_symbols[symbol] += 1
+        continue
+
+    absolute = int(symbol_match.group(1), 16)
+    candidates = [(start, pgoff) for start, pgoff in mmaps if absolute >= start]
+    if not candidates:
+        continue
+    start, pgoff = max(candidates, key=lambda pair: pair[0])
+    relative = absolute - start + pgoff
+    if relative >= 0:
+        counter[relative] += 1
+
+with output_file.open("w", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
+    fh.write(f"Post-symbolized user-space frames for {binary.name}\n")
+    fh.write(f"perf_data={perf_data}\n")
+    if mmaps:
+        fh.write("mmap_ranges=\n")
+        for start, pgoff in mmaps:
+            fh.write(f"- start={hex(start)} pgoff={hex(pgoff)}\n")
+    else:
+        fh.write("mmap_ranges=missing\n")
+    user_space_weight = sum(weighted_symbols.values()) + sum(counter.values())
+    fh.write(f"user_space_frames={user_space_weight}\n\n")
+
+    if not weighted_symbols and not counter:
+        fh.write("No user-space frames from the target binary were recovered.\n")
+        sys.exit(0)
+
+    fh.write("Top frames:\n")
+    combined = collections.Counter(weighted_symbols)
+    for relative, count in counter.items():
+        symbol = subprocess.check_output(
+            ["addr2line", "-Cfipe", str(binary), hex(relative)],
+            text=True,
+            errors="replace",
+        ).strip()
+        combined[f"{symbol} @ {hex(relative)}"] += count
+
+    total = sum(combined.values())
+    for symbol, count in combined.most_common(12):
+        percent = (count / total) * 100.0 if total else 0.0
+        fh.write(f"- {percent:5.1f}% ({count} frame hits)\n")
+        for line in symbol.splitlines():
+            fh.write(f"  {line}\n")
+PY
+}
